core_behavior.note: Model B introduces both idle and absolute timeout plus session extension and multi-device invalidation; Model A covers only idle timeout; Model C covers idle timeout plus unsaved data warning — all three make different unspecified design choices, and none fully address the unspecified timeout type or behavior per critical behaviors.

inputs.note: Model B specifies absolute timeout duration, warning lead time, qualifying activity definition, and session creation timestamp as distinct inputs not present in Models A or C, reflecting different unspecified design assumptions.

outputs.note: Model B includes audit log entry, HTTP 401 response, redirect-after-login URL preservation, and countdown timer; Model C includes unsaved data warning; Model A includes optional pop-up or countdown — each model introduces different unspecified output behaviors.

assumptions.note: Model B assumes server-side session storage with NTP-synchronized clocks, client polling, and HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite cookies; Model A assumes configurable admin timeout with a 30-minute default; Model C assumes client-side scripts communicate with server — these are incompatible unspecified design choices.

failure_modes.note: Model B has an empty failure_modes array while Models A and C both populate it with distinct failure scenarios.

edge_cases.note: Model B has an empty edge_cases array while Models A and C both populate it with distinct edge case scenarios.

security_constraints.note: Model B specifies opaque session IDs in HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite=Strict cookies and server-authoritative clock enforcement; Model A references OWASP/PCI-DSS compliance and session fixation prevention; Model C focuses on secure data clearing and tamper resistance — each introduces different unspecified security measures.

performance_constraints.note: Model B specifies a concrete polling interval default of 60 seconds via a named endpoint; Models A and C provide only general scalability and overhead statements without specific mechanisms or thresholds.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in 5 of the 8 fields, reflecting that the underspecified requirement caused all three models to independently introduce different unspecified design choices.
